question: We received a bid after the deadline and the supplier says they sent it on time. What proof counts, and what are we supposed to do with the offer?
must_include_concepts: late or delayed offers handling

operational next step or documentation

evaluation_focus: Operational handling of a common intake problem.
required_claims: The solicitation closing date and time are firm, and offerors are responsible for ensuring the offer reaches the proper location on time.
The only acceptable evidence of timely receipt at the specified offer receiving unit is the receipt issued by that specified offer receiving unit.
For Canada Post delays, only the specified CPC evidence is acceptable, and it must clearly show the offer was sent before the solicitation closing date and time.
Late offers delivered by methods other than CPC Connect service or other electronic delivery methods are returned unless they qualify as delayed offers.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide says the closing date and time are firm and the supplier is responsible for getting the offer to the correct location on time. For proof of timely receipt at the specified receiving unit, the acceptable evidence is the receipt issued by that unit. For Canada Post delays, only the listed CPC evidence is acceptable, and it must clearly show the offer was sent before closing. If the offer is simply late and does not qualify as a delayed offer, it should be returned rather than considered.

claim_evidence.claim: The solicitation closing date and time are firm and the supplier is responsible for timely delivery.
The only acceptable evidence of timely receipt is the receipt issued by the specified offer receiving unit.
Only the specified CPC evidence is acceptable for Canada Post delays and it must show the offer was sent before closing.
Late offers delivered by other methods are returned unless they qualify as delayed offers.

question: Our evaluation is slipping and the offer validity period will run out before we can award. What are our options if some suppliers agree to extend and others do not?

should_avoid: answering only with the normal validity-period rule
evaluation_focus: Timeline-sensitive Buyer’s Guide workflow.
required_claims: Canada may request in writing that all responsive offerors extend the offer validity period at least three days before it ends.
If all responsive offerors agree to the extension, the evaluation can continue under the extended validity period.
If not all responsive offerors agree, Canada may either continue evaluating only the offers from those who accepted the extension or cancel the solicitation.
If the offer acceptance period expires before contract award, the solicitation must be reissued, and any contract awarded after expiry is treated as a sole-source contract.
forbidden_claims: If the offer validity period expires before contract award, Canada can still award under the original solicitation without reissuing or sole-source justification.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide says Canada may request a written extension from all responsive offerors at least three days before the validity period ends. If all responsive offerors agree, the evaluation can continue under the extended period. If not all agree, Canada may either continue evaluating the offers from those who accepted the extension or cancel the solicitation. If the validity period expires before contract award, the solicitation must be reissued, and any post-expiry award is treated as a sole-source contract.

claim_evidence.claim: Canada may request a written extension from all responsive offerors at least three days before the end of the offer validity period.
If all responsive offerors accept the extension, the evaluation may continue under the extended period.
If all responsive offerors do not accept the extension, Canada may continue evaluating only those offers whose offerors accepted the extension or cancel the solicitation.
If the offer validity period expires before contract award, the solicitation must be reissued and any post-expiry award is considered sole-source.

The exceptions include things like PSIB certification and Canadian Content Policy certification.

question: We are thinking about posting an ACAN. What has to already be on file before we post it, and when should we not post one at all?

If there is no possibility of another business submitting an acceptable statement of capabilities, the contract should be awarded on a non-competitive basis with a contract award notice instead, and the rationale for not publishing an ACAN must be documented on file.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide says an ACAN needs key material on file before posting: a justification showing the applicable GCR exception and any relevant trade-agreement basis, plus a procurement plan or CPAA where applicable. It also says you should not post an ACAN if Canada cannot legitimately accept statements of capabilities from another business or if it is clear that more than one capable supplier exists and the ACAN would be used to bypass bidding. In situations where there is no real possibility of another acceptable statement of capabilities, the guide says to use a non-competitive process with a contract award notice and document the rationale for not publishing an ACAN.

Where one or more international trade agreements apply to a solicitation under a supply arrangement, an NPP must be published on GETS.

If CFTA applies, the qualified list may be used without publishing an NPP only if all offerors on the source list are invited and businesses can qualify on the list at any time, while the existence of the qualified list must still be published annually.
reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide says you start by determining trade agreement applicability based on things like value, covered entity, requirement type, and any exclusions or exceptions. The CFTA exception is narrower: if CFTA applies, you may use the qualified list without publishing an NPP only if all offerors on the source list are invited and businesses can qualify at any time, and the existence of the list still has to be published annually.

question: What is the actual contractual difference between a standing offer and a supply arrangement? When does Canada become legally bound under each one?

evaluation_focus: Cross-page synthesis question that a real client might ask in plain language.

reference_answer: The Buyer’s Guide distinguishes the two clearly. A standing offer is not itself a contract; Canada becomes bound when it issues a call-up, and each call-up is a separate contract. A supply arrangement also does not create a contract at the arrangement stage. Canada becomes bound only when it awards an individual contract under the arrangement, and each such contract is a separate binding contract.

claim_evidence.claim: A standing offer is not itself a contract, and each call-up creates a separate contract.
There is no contractual obligation at the supply arrangement stage.
Each contract awarded under a supply arrangement is a separate binding contract.

question: We want to reduce the solicitation period to 15 days after a prior request for expressions of interest. When is that allowed, what had to be in the earlier notice, and do we still have to give suppliers enough time anyway?
must_include_concepts: 15-day reduction conditions
earlier NPP content requirements

should_avoid: treating the 15-day period as automatic whenever there was a prior notice
omitting the continuing duty to give suppliers enough time
evaluation_focus: Workflow branch for shortening a solicitation period after an REOI without dropping the reasonableness constraint.
required_claims: A solicitation following a request for expressions of interest may be reduced to 15 calendar days only if one or more international trade agreements apply and the earlier NPP was published at least 40 days, but no more than 12 months, before the new NPP.

The earlier NPP must have included a description of the procurement, approximate final dates for offers or requests to participate, a request that businesses express interest, the address for obtaining the procurement documents, and as much of the new NPP information as was available.
Even when a minimum solicitation-period rule applies, buyers must still give offerors enough time to prepare and submit compliant offers.
reference_answer: You can use the 15-day period only if the file is subject to one or more international trade agreements and the earlier notice was published at least 40 days but no more than 12 months before the new NPP. That earlier notice also had to include the core procurement description, timing, expression-of-interest request, document address, and as much of the new-notice information as was available. Even then, the Buyer’s Guide still says you must give offerors enough time to prepare and submit compliant offers.

claim_evidence.claim: A solicitation following a request for expressions of interest may be reduced to 15 calendar days only if one or more international trade agreements apply and the earlier NPP was published at least 40 days, but no more than 12 months, before the new NPP.

question: A company is not showing up on the controlled-goods public list. Can I rely on that list alone, and does that page give me the exact CGD email address or phone number?

question: The Buy Canadian page mentions a policy framework, but for governance who actually owns that framework and what are contracting authorities required to do with it?
must_include_concepts: PSPC owns framework development and implementation
contracting authorities must follow framework processes
document decisions and respond to PSPC data requests
should_avoid: treating the Buy Canadian overview page as the governance source
answering only with generic statements about supporting Canadian industry
evaluation_focus: Authority-split case where the TBS directive, not the overview page, controls the governance answer.
required_claims: The TBS directive says Public Services and Procurement Canada is responsible for developing, maintaining, and implementing the Buy Canadian Procurement Policy Framework, including issuing associated policy instruments, programs, guidance materials, tools, and training.
The TBS directive says contracting authorities must adhere to the processes set out in the Buy Canadian Procurement Policy Framework in procurements and document decisions regarding its application.
The TBS directive says contracting authorities must respond to PSPC requests for data concerning the application of the Buy Canadian Procurement Policy Framework.
reference_answer: For governance, the controlling source is the TBS directive. It says PSPC owns developing, maintaining, and implementing the Buy Canadian Procurement Policy Framework and issuing the related instruments and guidance. It also says contracting authorities have to follow the framework’s processes in procurements, document their decisions about applying it, and respond to PSPC data requests about that application.

question: Our strategy changed, the risk has moved up, and the procurement now looks like complexity level 3. Before award, what do we need to redo, what has to go into the contract request, and what authority level does that combination point to?
must_include_concepts: redo changed assessments
new PRA and updated complexity package
medium-high risk level 3 authority
should_avoid: assuming the original approval package remains valid after material changes
giving the wrong delegated authority for medium-high risk at complexity level 3
evaluation_focus: Cross-page operational synthesis on changed risk and complexity before contract award.
required_claims: When procurement strategy or new information changes the risk or complexity picture, buyers must realign the assessments and may need to perform them again and resubmit the approval document to the appropriate authority level.

If risks have changed, the contract-request process requires a new Procurement Risk Assessment that identifies the changed risks and explains the reason for the change and the mitigation strategy; if the complexity level changed, the complexity assessment must be updated and a new PRA at the appropriate complexity level must be attached before finalizing the contract request.
Under the risk-based procurement delegation grid, medium-high risk at complexity level 3 points to Senior director, Regional director, or Director.
For complexity levels 2 and 3, the Procurement Risk Assessment may require a formal peer review of evaluation results before contract award.
reference_answer: You cannot just rely on the original package. The Buyer’s Guide says changes in strategy or new information can require the complexity and risk assessments to be redone and the approval document resubmitted to the appropriate authority. In the contract request, changed risks require a new PRA with the new risks, reasons, and mitigation, and a changed complexity level requires an updated complexity assessment plus a new PRA at the right level before the request is finalized. For medium-high risk at complexity level 3, the delegation grid points to Senior director, Regional director, or Director. The evaluation page also says a formal peer review may be required for levels 2 and 3 depending on the PRA.

question: We are planning a PSIB set-aside that may touch CLCA obligations and could end up over $2 million. What do we need to check first, when do we notify ISC, and what has to happen before and after award?
must_include_concepts: CLCA precedence
ISC notification before release
top-two and $2M pre-award audit workflow
should_avoid: ignoring the CLCA conflict check
saying ISC only gets involved after award
evaluation_focus: Operational Indigenous-procurement sequence spanning CLCA analysis, ISC notification, and pre-award audit handling.
required_claims: For a PSIB set-aside, buyers must assess whether the procurement is subject to Comprehensive Land Claims Agreements and the extent to which the CLCAs conflict with the set-aside, and the CLCAs take precedence.

Once a PSIB set-aside requisition is received and accepted, ISC must be notified before the solicitation is released, and the notification must include the estimated dollar value, description, solicitation number, solicitation closing date, and buyer contact details.
Before award, ISC must be informed of the two best-assessed responsive offerors, excluding financial information.

For requirements valued at $2 million or more, a pre-award audit of the offeror’s certification is mandatory, the top two best-assessed offers must go to ISC for eligibility confirmation, and the contract must not be awarded until ISC confirms the proposed contractor’s eligibility.
Within 15 working days after contract award, ISC must be advised of the award.
reference_answer: Start by checking whether the procurement is subject to any CLCAs and whether they conflict with the PSIB set-aside, because the CLCAs take precedence. Once the PSIB set-aside requisition is accepted, ISC has to be notified before release with the file basics, including value, description, solicitation number, closing date, and buyer contact details. Before award, ISC has to be told who the two best-assessed responsive offerors are, and if the requirement is worth $2 million or more, the pre-award audit becomes mandatory, the top two offers have to go to ISC for eligibility confirmation, and no award can happen until ISC confirms eligibility. ISC also has to be advised of the award within 15 working days afterward.

question: If we want to use PSPC emergency contracting limits, what trade-agreement and approval conditions have to be met, what do we have to report, and when does TB approval or ratification become necessary?
must_include_concepts: trade-agreement urgency or exception condition
60-day reporting
over-$25M TB approval and ratification
should_avoid: treating emergency authority as available without documenting the trade-agreement basis
omitting the reporting or ratification steps
evaluation_focus: Emergency-contracting authority case spanning criteria, reporting, and ratification.
required_claims: To use PSPC emergency contracting limits, the extreme-urgency provision of each applicable trade agreement must have been applied or an exception to each applicable trade agreement must apply, the requirement cannot be met through normal contracting procedures due to urgency, and the applicable department must provide the Minister’s or delegated approval to use the special authorities.

The file documentation must include the explanation of the urgency and rationale for using emergency contracting limits, and the details of the extreme-urgency provision or trade-agreement exception that was applied.
An emergency contracting report must be submitted to the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat within 60 calendar days of entering into or amending an emergency contract or contractual arrangement.

Treasury Board approval is required for any emergency contract or contractual arrangement over $25 million, and if such a contract is entered into, ratification from the Treasury Board must be requested as soon as possible.
reference_answer: Using PSPC’s emergency contracting limits is not just a general urgency call. The Buyer’s Guide says each applicable trade agreement needs an extreme-urgency basis or exception, the requirement has to be impossible to meet through normal procedures because of urgency, and the client department has to approve the use of the special authority. The documentation has to record both the urgency rationale and the trade-agreement basis, and the emergency contracting report has to go to TBS within 60 calendar days. If the emergency contract is over $25 million, TB approval is required, and if the contract has already been entered into, you then have to seek TB ratification as soon as possible.

question: A client requirement is subject to CLCA obligations, but the existing mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement does not address them. Do we still have to use the mandatory instrument, and what if we want a non-CLCA instrument for other work?
must_include_concepts: mandatory instruments are the default
CLCA-based exemption
non-CLCA instrument clause and new-procurement fallback
should_avoid: saying the mandatory instrument must always be used even when it does not meet CLCA obligations
omitting the restriction on deliveries or services in CLCA settlement areas
evaluation_focus: Method-of-supply exception case for CLCA obligations and mandatory instruments.
required_claims: Mandatory standing offers and supply arrangements must be used before any new procurement is considered.

If a client requirement is subject to CLCA contracting obligations and no mandatory standing offer or supply arrangement addresses those obligations, the client department is exempt from using the mandatory instrument.

If no or few resulting contracts or task authorizations will be subject to CLCAs, it may be appropriate to issue an instrument that excludes CLCA provisions, but that instrument must include a clause stating that deliveries and services cannot occur within CLCA settlement areas.
If a future requirement arises that is subject to CLCAs and no instrument exists that meets the applicable CLCA obligations, the requirement must be handled as a new procurement.
reference_answer: Mandatory standing offers and supply arrangements are still the starting point, but the Buyer’s Guide says the client is exempt from using one if the requirement is subject to CLCA obligations and no mandatory instrument addresses those obligations. If you create an instrument for work that is not expected to fall within those obligations, it has to include a clause saying deliveries and services cannot occur within CLCA settlement areas. And if a future CLCA-affected requirement comes up with no suitable instrument, that requirement has to be handled as a new procurement.
